messages.content: random thing i've always wondered, why do we get hiccups? like what's the actual point of them

Honestly it's one of those things where the answer is kind of unsatisfying - hiccups don't really seem to have a useful purpose *now*, but there are some interesting theories about why we have the reflex at all.

The most popular idea is that it's basically an evolutionary leftover. The thinking goes that our ancient ancestors - like really ancient, pre-mammal ancestors - had gills, and the hiccup reflex might be related to a breathing pattern that helped pump water over gills. The specific muscle movement (diaphragm contracts sharply, then your airway snaps shut) doesn't make much sense for air breathing but does kind of match gill ventilation in fish and amphibians.

There's also a theory connecting it to how mammals feed - the reflex might have helped infant mammals clear air from their stomachs while nursing, kind of like a forced burp mechanism.

But honestly neither theory is super conclusively proven. It's one of those biological quirks that researchers find genuinely puzzling.

What's pretty well understood is the *mechanism* - your diaphragm gets irritated or spasms (often from eating too fast, carbonation, temperature changes) and you get that involuntary contraction. The "hic" sound is just your vocal cords snapping shut right after.

The fact that we have no reliable cure for them kind of suggests your body doesn't really know what to do with them either 😄
my niece had them forever yesterday and it got me curious about the whole thing

Aw poor kid, prolonged hiccups are so miserable! There's something especially pitiful about watching a little one just getting hiccuped repeatedly and not being able to do anything about it 😄

Did they eventually just stop on their own? That's usually how it goes - your body sort of resets itself eventually.

It's funny because hiccups are one of those things where everyone has their own "guaranteed cure" that they swear by. Holding your breath, drinking water upside down, getting scared, breathing into a bag... and yet none of them are actually proven to work consistently. They probably just give your body something else to focus on, or buy enough time for the spasm to stop naturally.

For kids they can be pretty frequent too since they eat quickly and their little diaphragms are easily irritated. Totally normal but definitely annoying for everyone involved!

How old is your niece? Is she at the age where hiccups are kind of funny to her or was she genuinely bothered by them?
i swear everyone's got their own folk cure too. my grandma always did the spoonful of sugar thing

The spoonful of sugar one is actually kind of interesting because there might be some logic behind it! The theory is that swallowing something with a strong texture or taste can stimulate the vagus nerve, which is involved in the hiccup reflex, and kind of "reset" it. So it's not completely random folk wisdom.

Mary Poppins would approve either way 😄

It's so funny how these things get passed down though - your grandma probably got it from her grandma and so on, and nobody really questions it because sometimes it works and that's enough. Classic confirmation bias in action too, like if you try it and the hiccups stop you remember the cure worked, but if they stop on their own two minutes later without any sugar you also kind of credit whatever you last tried.

The holding your breath one is probably the most widespread and that one at least has a decent explanation - building up CO2 in your lungs can calm diaphragm spasms. So there's something there.

Did your grandma's sugar trick actually seem to work or was it more of a "worth a try" situation? I feel like with kids especially half the value of any cure is just the distraction of doing something about it 😄
it's kinda funny how something so common is apparently still a bit of a mystery science-wise

Right?? And it's not even like it's some rare obscure thing - literally every person on earth gets hiccups, scientists have had forever to study them, and we still can't fully explain why they exist or reliably stop them. That's kind of wild when you think about it.

It's actually a good reminder that a lot of really mundane everyday things are still not fully understood. Like yawning is another one - super common, happens to everyone, and researchers are still debating why we do it. The contagious yawning thing especially is still pretty mysterious.

I think there's a tendency to assume science has figured out all the "basic" stuff and is now working on the complicated things, but actually some of the most ordinary human experiences still have big question marks around them.

Hiccups are almost a bit humbling in that way - your body is doing this thing completely outside your control, you can't reliably stop it, and nobody's entirely sure what the point is. You're basically just along for the ride 😄

It's the kind of thing that sounds like a weird fact at a dinner party - "you know scientists don't actually know why we hiccup" - and people kind of don't believe you at first because it seems too basic to still be a mystery.
